Certain other points taken, which are not material to this report, and a special ca as well as on the others. The other points b decided against the prisoners, the conviction was sustained ; J Lilliecrap and another (1).

The present application was for special leave to appeal fi that decision. Garland, for the applicants. Where a special verdict is retu it is necessary that the jury should state in their finding all essential elements of the offence of which they intend to eon the accused.

If they do not clearly state these matters, impossible to say whether they have really considered and found them against the accused. There should be no u tainty in the verdict. If the words used are consistent innocence, i.¢., are capable of meaning something which | offence in law, the verdict is bad.

FitH C.J. The point taken in this case is a very simple The prisoners were charged with larceny and with receivg. Under the Statute a person charged with larceny may be victed of an offence under sec. 131, i.c., of taking and' working erwise using cattle the property of another person without ent of the owner or person in lawful possession thereof, may be punished accordingly.

At the trial of the prisoners learned Judge in his summing up told the jury that they y thought that the cattle were only taken for the purpose of ng them, they might find a verdict of "illegally using." The y acquitted the accused of stealing and of receiving, and md them guilty of "illegally using." The objection taken that the verdict ought to have been returned in the terms section, i.¢., guilty of "illegally taking and using" the in question. A special case having been stated, it was d before the Supreme Court that in a special verdict Jury must use the precise words of the section describing the But that Court took the common sense view that it is sufficient if the words used in the oral verdict are those comused to describe the particular offence. It is not disputed words used were those under which this offence is comy known.
